coredata.dc:title: A robust Eu-MOF as a multi-functional fluorescence sensor for detection of benzaldehyde, Hg2+, and Cr2O7 2-/CrO4 2-
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Linhan
Zhang, Jingyue
Zhao, Yun
Sun, Changyan
Li, Wenjun
Chang, Zhidong
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel Eu-MOF, {[Eu2(L)(phen)2(ox)2(H2O)2]·10H2O·phen}n (H2L = 2,6-bis(4-carboxyphenyl)pyrazine, phen = 1,10-phenanthroline), was assembled under hydrothermal conditions with good water stability and pH stability. The Eu-MOF could be used as a luminescent sensor for detecting gas/liquid benzaldehyde, Hg2+ and Cr2O7 2-/CrO4 2- with high sensitivity and selectivity based on luminescence quenching effects. The sensing mechanisms were systematically studied by ICP-AES, PXRD analysis and UV–vis spectroscopy. In addition, the Eu-MOF shows high adsorption of Hg2+ and can be successfully used to quantitatively detect Hg2+ in real samples such as tap water, green tea, and river water. The sensor exhibited a recovery rate in the range of 99.84 %∼102.34 % for detecting Hg2+. The portable fluorescent paper strips based on the Eu-MOF were also successfully fabricated, which can detect benzaldehyde both in gas and liquid phases. The fluorescence sensing system based on Eu-MOF provides a reference for the construction of portable environmental pollutant monitoring sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Eu-MOF
Fluorescence probes
Fast responsive detection
Low detection limit
Gas-sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of test stripes for detection of benzaldehyde
Mechanisms of detecting benzaldehyde, Hg2+ and Cr2O7 2-/CrO4 2-
Detection of Hg2+ in real samples
Detection of Cr2O7 2-/CrO4 2- in real samples
Potential application as a paper-based sensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Foundation for key young teachers of University of Science and Technology Beijing
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project is supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21101013 ) and Foundation for key young teachers of University of Science and Technology Beijing ( 2302019JXGGRC-003 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The coordination environment of Eu3+ ions in Eu-MOF (H atoms omitted for clarity); (b) Asymmetric unit; (c) The 2D layer constructed by the 1D chain; (d) The 3D network.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of Eu-MOF dispersed in different solvents (λex = 360 nm). (b) Fluorescence intensity of Eu-MOF without/with benzaldehyde in different solvents. (c) Fluorescence intensity of Eu-MOF with different concentrations of benzaldehyde. (d) Fitted nonlinear SV plot of Eu-MOF for benzaldehyde by using the exponential quenching equation (Inset, SV plot of benzaldehyde in low concentration).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of Eu-MOF dispersed in dispersed in aqueous solutions with various metal ions (λex = 360 nm). (b) Fluorescence intensity of Eu-MOF without/with Hg2+ in aqueous solutions with various metal ions. (c) Fluorescence intensity of Eu-MOF with different concentrations of Hg2+. (d) Fitted nonlinear SV plot of Eu-MOF for Hg2+ by using the exponential quenching equation (Inset, SV plot of Hg2+ in low concentration).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of Eu-MOF dispersed in dispersed in aqueous solutions with various anions (λex = 360 nm). Fluorescence intensity of Eu-MOF without/with Cr2O7 2-(b) and CrO4 2-(c) in aqueous solutions with various anions. (d) Fluorescence intensity of Eu-MOF with different concentrations of Cr2O7 2-. (e) Fitted nonlinear SV plot of Eu-MOF for Cr2O7 2- by using the exponential quenching equation (Inset, SV plot of Cr2O7 2- in low concentration). (f) Fluorescence intensity of Eu-MOF with different concentrations of CrO4 2-. (h) Fitted nonlinear SV plot of Eu-MOF for CrO4 2- by using the exponential quenching equation (Inset, SV plot of CrO4 2- in low concentration).

(a) Preparation process of the test strip and the luminescence quenching phenomenon of the test strip caused by benzaldehyde. (b) The prepared test strip under daylight, under 365 nm UV lamp before and after adding benzaldehyde. (c) Color change of prepared test strips with different solvents (under a 365 nm UV lamp). (d) Color change of the prepared test strips under daylight and 365 nm UV light before and after exposed to volatile benzaldehyde vapor. (d) Fluorescence images of test papers after the reaction with different concentrations of Hg2+ (0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120 and 140 μM) under 365 nm UV lamp. (e) The schematic diagram of the designed smartphone application for Hg2+ detection. (f) Linear relationship between B/R values and Hg2+ concentrations (0–140 μM).
Determination of Hg2+ in real samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Recovery:(A/B) × 100 % (A: Concentration of added analyte, B: Concentration of the analyte using fluorescence detection method).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of Cr2O7 2-/CrO4 2- in real samples.

head.ce:title: A robust Eu-MOF as a multi-functional fluorescence sensor for detection of benzaldehyde, Hg2+, and Cr2O7 2-/CrO4 2-

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Biological Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Biological Engineering
University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Biological Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new metal–organic framework (Eu-MOF) was synthesized with good water stability and pH stability and it could be used to detect gas/liquid benzaldehyde, Hg2+ and Cr2O7 2-/CrO4 2-. In addition, this Eu-MOF displays good analytical accuracy in the detection of Hg2+ in real samples such as tap water, green tea and river water. And the Eu-MOF can also achieve high adsorption of Hg2+. The fluorescent portable paper strips based on the Eu-MOF were prepared, which can detect benzaldehyde in gas and liquid phases.

In this work, a novel Eu-MOF, {[Eu2(L)(phen)2(ox)2(H2O)2]·10H2O·phen}n (H2L = 2,6-bis(4-carboxyphenyl)pyrazine, phen = 1,10-phenanthroline), was assembled under hydrothermal conditions with good water stability and pH stability. The Eu-MOF could be used as a luminescent sensor for detecting gas/liquid benzaldehyde, Hg2+ and Cr2O7 2-/CrO4 2- with high sensitivity and selectivity based on luminescence quenching effects. The sensing mechanisms were systematically studied by ICP-AES, PXRD analysis and UV–vis spectroscopy. In addition, the Eu-MOF shows high adsorption of Hg2+ and can be successfully used to quantitatively detect Hg2+ in real samples such as tap water, green tea, and river water. The sensor exhibited a recovery rate in the range of 99.84 %∼102.34 % for detecting Hg2+. The portable fluorescent paper strips based on the Eu-MOF were also successfully fabricated, which can detect benzaldehyde both in gas and liquid phases. The fluorescence sensing system based on Eu-MOF provides a reference for the construction of portable environmental pollutant monitoring sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are a unique class of promising materials that are composed of inorganic ions/clusters and organic ligands through coordination bonds, which self-assembles into networks with potential voids [1–4]. As one kind of porous material with large surface area, high porosity, and diverse structure [5,6], MOFs have been intensively studied in different scientific fields, including magnetics [7], gas storage and separation [8–11], heterogeneous catalysis [12,13] and fluorescence sensing [14–16]. Luminescent MOFs (LMOFs) have been extensively used to detect organic compounds [17–19], anions and cations [20–22] for the advantages of simple operation, high detection sensitivity, time saving and low cost, which has attracted wide attention in recent years.

As one kind of widely used chemical raw material, benzaldehyde will cause serious environmental pollution and have a seriously effect on human health [23–25]. Volatile benzaldehyde is considered to be one of the toxic VOCs [26]. Long-term exposure to gaseous benzaldehyde will cause neurotoxicity and genetic toxicity to the human body, and greatly increase the risk of anemia and leukemia [27,28]. Therefore, it is urgent to develop methods to detect gaseous and liquid benzaldehyde. Currently, it has been reported that MOFs can be used to detect benzaldehyde. For example, Wang et al. [29] synthesized a pH stable three-dimensional Tb-MOF (JXUST-19). Fluorescence experiments have shown that JXUST-19 can be used as a rare on–off and blue shift MOF sensor for benzaldehyde and salicylaldehyde. This is the first MOF-based on–off fluorescence probe which could be observed by naked eyes to detect benzaldehyde. However, the detection of gaseous benzaldehyde based on MOFs fluorescence probes has not been reported so far. Mercury ions (Hg2+) and chromium anions Cr2O7 2-/CrO4 2- are widely used in industrial production processes, and they are also widely distributed environmental pollutants [30–32]. Excessive intake of Hg2+ and Cr2O7 2-/CrO4 2- can lead to a range of chronic diseases even cancers [33,34]. Therefore, the detection of these pollutants is very important. Guo et al. [30] reported the preparation of a dual emission material Eu-Ca-MOF which has excellent photoluminescence performance and can be used as a ratio fluorescence sensor (I381/I590) to sensitively detect Hg2+ions in water. Xiao et al. [35] synthesized a multi-responsive metal–organic framework (MOF), formulated as [Zn(tpbpc)2]·solvent which can detect Hg2+, CrO4 2- and Cr2O7 2- ions and realize the visual detection of Hg2+. However, up to now, there have been no reports of distinguishing volatile benzaldehyde, Hg2+, and Cr2O7 2-/CrO4 2- simultaneously based on MOFs.

In this paper, 2,6-bis(4-carboxyphenyl)pyrazine (H2L) was chosen to construct MOF for the following reasons: (1) The large π electron conjugation system makes it possible to form MOFs with good fluorescence performance [36,37]. (2) The numerous O/N atoms could be not only involved in coordination, but also potential sites to sense a variety of substances [38]. (3) The rigid aromatic structure can provide a scaffold to support the entire skeleton structure during the coordination process, making the structure more stable [39]. The fluorescence sensing experiments show that the synthesized Eu-MOF can be used as a highly selective and sensitive fluorescent probe to detect volatile benzaldehyde, Hg2+ and Cr2O7 2-/CrO4 2- by fluorescence quenching. The possible mechanisms were discussed in detail. Importantly, this method can be successfully applied to detect Hg2+ in real samples. The Eu-MOF visual test paper was also prepared for the detection of benzaldehyde in both gas and liquid phases.

The experimental reagents were all directly purchased from commercial company and they were at least analytical grade and without further purification. 2,6-bis (4-carboxyphenyl) pyrazine was purchased from EXTENSION. Eu(NO3)3·6H2O (99.9%), sodium oxalate (Na2ox) and 1,10-phenanthroline (phen) were purchased from Adamas. Deionized water was prepared in laboratory.

Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) data for all samples were collected at room temperature on bulk samples with Cu-Kα radiation (1.54059 Å) on a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer, in which the X-ray tube was operated at 40 kV and 40 mA. Crystal structure data via graphite monochrome light source MoKα (λ = 0.71073 Å) were collected and used on the Bruker D8 Venture X-ray single crystal diffractometer. FT-IR spectra were recorded in the range of 500–4000 cm−1 on Nicolet iS10 using KBr pellets. Fluorescence spectra are measured on Hitachi F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer, and UV–vis spectra are measured on Shimadzu UV2600 spectrophotometer.

A mixture of Eu(NO3)3·6H2O (0.05 mmol, 0.0223 g), H2L (0.1 mmol, 0.0320 g), phen (0.1 mmol, 18.0 mg), Na2ox (0.05 mmol, 6.7 mg), and NaOH (0.25 mL, 1 mol/L) in H2O (5 mL) was sealed in a 25 mL Teflon lined stainless container, heated to 120 °C for 72 h. Upon cooling to ambient temperature with a descent rate of 5 °C·h−1, prismatic colorless crystals were obtained. Yield: 63.0 % (based on Eu). Anal. Calcd for C58H58Eu2N8O24: C, 44.76; N, 7.20; H, 3.73; Found: C, 44.77; N, 6.98; H, 4.55. IR (KBr pellet, cm−1): 3315 (w), 1664 (s), 1608 (s),1590 (s), 1537 (s), 1393 (vs), 1307 (w), 1279 (w), 1178 (m), 1101 (m), 1016 (m), 902 (w), 866 (m), 784 (s), 743 (w), 701 (s), 664 (m), 626 (w), 566 (w), 530 (m),520 (m).

The luminescence properties of Eu-MOF in solid state and various solvents were investigated at room temperature. 3 mg Eu-MOF crystalline powders were added to 3 mL different solvents, including H2O, methanol (MeOH), ethanol (EtOH), isopropanol (i-PrOH), toluene, CH2Cl2, CHCl3, CCl4, CH3CN, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), N, N'-dimethylacetamide (DMA), tetrahydrofuran (THF), cyclohexane, formaldehyde, benzene and benzaldehyde. The mixtures were treated by sonication for 30 min to form a stable suspension for fluorescence studies. The detection of cations and anions were conducted with the similar procedure. 3 mg Eu-MOF powders were dispersed in 3 mL aqueous solutions of MClx (Mx+ = K+, Li+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni+, Sr2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Hg2+, 1 × 10-3 mol/L) or KX (X = Cl-, Br-, I-, NO3 –, ClO-, SO4 2-, CrO4 2-, Cr2O7 2-, C2O4 2-, CH3COO–, F-, SO3 2-, S2O3 2-, 1 × 10-3 mol/L).

Crystallographic data for Eu-MOF were collected using the Bruker D8 Venture diffractometer with a photon detector under graphite monochrome Mo-Kα radiation (λ = 0.71073 Å). Full-matrix least-squares technique on F2 with the SHELXTL-97 process was used to solve the structure. Then, the non-hydrogen coordinates and anisotropy parameters are refined by the full matrix least square method. More details of the X-ray structural analyses are summarized in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of test stripes for detection of benzaldehyde
ce:section.ce:para: 30 mg Eu-MOF powder was dispersed in 15 mL EtOH. After 20 min of ultrasound, the Whatman filter paper (1 × 3 cm2) was immersed in the uniformly dispersed Eu-MOF suspension and dried for 30 min at room temperature. Repeat this procedure three times to get fluorescent test strips. For the detection of benzaldehyde in the liquid phase, different solvents were dropped onto the paper, and the fluorescence color changes were observed under 365 nm UV lamp.

The detection of gas benzaldehyde was carried out by exposing fluorescent test strips to a saturated medium of benzaldehyde vapor, which was obtained by adding 2 mL benzaldehyde in a closed cuvette. Fluorescence color changes were observed under 365 nm UV lamp.

The detection of Hg2+ in tap water, green tea and Beijing Wanquan River water was further studied. Before the experiment, 2 g green tea were soaked in 50 mL deionized water for one day. The collected water samples were treated with a 0.22 μm filter, and the pH was adjusted to 5–6. A series of different amounts of Hg2+ standard solutions were added to the sample solutions and the fluorescence spectra were recorded.

Evaluate the practicality of the established method for detecting Cr2O7 2-/CrO4 2- using milk and tap water samples. Milk samples were purchased from supermarkets, diluted with deionized water (100 mL) and centrifuged at 10000 rad/min for 15 min. The supernatant is diluted for fluorescence sensing experiments. Add different concentrations of Cr2O7 2-/CrO4 2- solutions to the treated milk and tap water, and record the changes in fluorescence emission.

Single crystal X-ray diffraction analysis reveals that Eu-MOF is a 3D network, which crystallizes in the triclinic crystal system with the centrosymmetric P-1 space group. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the asymmetric structural unit comprises two crystal independent Eu(III) ions (Eu1 and Eu2). Each Eu(III) is nine coordinated with two oxygen atoms in an H2L ligand, two nitrogen atoms in a phen ligand, two oxygen atoms in an oxalate (ox) ligand, and one oxygen atom in a coordinated water molecule, forming a distorted single capped tetragonal antiprism (Fig. 1b). But the bond lengths and angles of Eu1 and Eu2 are different. Eu1-O ranges from 2.4022(35) to 2.5579(28) Å, while Eu2-O ranges from 2.4116(46) to 2.5042(44). O-Eu1-O is within the range of 52.648°-145.574°, while O-Eu2-O is within the range of 52.429°-149.432°. Adjacent Eu1 and Eu2 are connected by a four-dentate ox ligand to form a dinuclear structure, in which the Eu1⋯Eu2 distance is 6.1994 Å. Another configuration of four-dentate ox ligands bridge the dinuclear structures to form one-dimensional chains (Eu1⋯Eu2 distances, 6.1994 Å, 6.3158 Å). These chains were connected by chelation bidentate carboxylate of H2L ligands, forming 2D layer structures (Fig. 1c). There are π⋯π stacking interactions (face-to-face distance 3.3654 Å) between phen ligands of the adjacent layer structures, which results in the formation of a 3D supramolecular network (Fig. 1d). The analysis shows that the synthesized Eu-MOF has a porous structure, and the nitrogen atoms on the pyrazine rings in H2L ligands are not involved in coordination, which provides the possibility for subsequent fluorescence detection.

The solid-state photoluminescence properties of Eu-MOF and free H2L at room temperature were studied. As shown in Fig. S1, the emission spectrum of H2L has a broad band centered at 423 nm (λex = 325 nm), which may be due to the intramolecular π*-π electron transition. Strong red emission could be observed in Eu-MOF under ultraviolet light (Fig. 2 inset). There are two sharp emission peaks at 593 and 618 nm, corresponding to the transitions of 5D0 → 7F1 and 5D0 → 7F2 of Eu(III) ions.

The stability of Eu-MOF in different pH aqueous solutions was studied. Eu-MOF samples were soaked in a series of acid-alkaline aqueous solutions with different pH values (2–12) for 24 h, then the samples were filtered and dried in the air, and PXRD patterns were measured, as shown in Fig. S2. There are almost no significant changes in the PXRD patterns in the pH range of 4–11, indicating the high stability of Eu-MOF in a wide pH range. In addition, the PXRD spectrum of Eu-MOF immersed in water for five days are consistent with the original PXRD spectrum, indicating that the structure of Eu-MOF remains stable after soaking in water for five days. These results show that Eu-MOF has high stability.

The luminescent stability is key to the application of luminescent sensors. Therefore, the luminescence behaviors of Eu-MOF at different pH were further studied. Eu-MOF (3 mg) was suspended in 3 mL solution with pH value of 2–13. Then the luminescence intensity was recorded under 360 nm excitation. In the pH range of 4–11, the fluorescence intensity is basically the same, as shown in Fig. S3a. When the pH value is less than 4 or greater than 11, the luminescence intensity changes greatly. The results show that the luminescence of Eu-MOF can maintain stable in pH ranges of 4–11. At the same time, as shown in Fig. S3b, the fluorescence of Eu-MOF remained stable after immersion in water for five days.

The luminescence properties of Eu-MOF in different solvent suspensions (H2O, MeOH, EtOH, CCl4, DMSO, DMA, THF, formaldehyde, benzene and benzaldehyde, etc.) were measured. As shown in Fig. 2a, it is obvious that solvents can affect the emission intensity of Eu-MOF. Compared to the other solvents used in this study, only benzaldehyde resulted in nearly 100 % luminescence quenching, which indicates that Eu-MOF can be used as a good fluorescence sensor for selective identification of benzaldehyde. In practical applications, chemical sensors need not only high selectivity, but also good anti-interference. As shown in Fig. 2b, significant fluorescence quenching can also be observed after adding benzaldehyde to the suspension of Eu-MOF containing multiple interfering substances, which supports the good anti-interference ability of the sensor.

Considering benzaldehyde is insoluble in water, another solvent must be selected for benzaldehyde detection. It can be seen from Fig. S4 that the luminescence of Eu-MOF is relatively stable in ethanol. So, ethanol is selected as the solvent for the sensing of benzaldehyde. The regeneration cycle test was carried out. At the end of each fluorescence test, the Eu-MOF was centrifuged for washing and air drying, and then reused. As shown in Fig. S5, the fluorescence intensity of Eu-MOF remained basically unchanged after five cycles, and the fluorescence quenching also occurred with the addition of benzaldehyde, indicating good recyclability. The results indicate that Eu-MOF can be used as a fluorescent probe for detecting benzaldehyde with high selectivity, excellent anti-interference ability and good recyclability.

Subsequently, the emission spectra of the Eu-MOF ethanol suspensions containing different concentrations of benzaldehyde were tested in order to investigate the quantitative detection ability of Eu-MOF for benzaldehyde. As shown in Fig. 2c, the fluorescence intensity of Eu-MOF ethanol suspension decreased gradually upon the addition of benzaldehyde. The fitted result is in accordance with the Stern-Volmer (SV) equation (I/I0 = 1 + KSV[C]). As displayed in Fig. 2c, the fitted line shows a satisfactory correlation (R2 = 0.9935) at a low concentration range (0–2 × 10-4M), and the quenching coefficient (Ksv) was calculated 4.71 × 104 M−1. The limit of detection (LOD) is calculated to be 4.23 × 10-8 M according to the equation 3σ/k [40] (Fig. S6). At present, it is comparable or better than most of the previously reported MOF-based fluorescent probes for the detection of benzaldehyde (Table S2) [41–44].

Similar method was used to study the selection for metal ions. Metal cations (K+, Li+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni+, Sr2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Hg2+, 1 × 10-3 mol/L) with Cl- as anions were selected for selective detection. The addition of different metal ions to the suspension containing Eu-MOF showed that it had no significant effect on the luminescence intensity of the suspension containing other metal ions. However, the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly with the addition of Hg2+. (Fig. 3 a). Furthermore, the anti-jamming experiment was carried out (Fig. 3b). Fluorescence quenching could still be achieved in the presence of interfering substances when Hg2+ was added. To unambiguously establish the relationship between Hg2+ concentration and luminescence quenching efficiency, the Hg2+ concentration titration test was carried out on the water suspension of Eu-MOF. In the range of 0–4.0 × 10-7M, there is a good linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and Hg2+ concentration (R2 = 0.9981), and the quenching coefficient (Ksv) was calculated 7.56 × 105 M−1 (Fig. 3c, d). Based on 3σ/K, the LOD was calculated 1.50 × 10-9M (Fig. S7). Remarkably, Eu-MOF shows lower LOD than most previously reported MOF-based materials for the detection of Hg2+ ions (Table S3) [45–49].

The effect of anions on the fluorescence emission of Eu-MOF was tested. Finely ground samples of Eu-MOF were dispersed in aqueous solution with different potassium salts KX (X = Cl-, Br-, I-, NO3 –, ClO-, SO4 2-, CrO4 2-, Cr2O7 2−, C2O4 2-, CH3COO–, F-, SO3 2-, S2O3 2-, 1 × 10-3 mol/L) (3 mL). The results of the experiments are shown in Fig. 4 a. Among the selected anions, only Cr2O7 2- and CrO4 2- significantly quenched the fluorescence intensity of Eu-MOF. And the presence of other anions has little effect on the detection of Cr2O7 2-/CrO4 2- (Fig. 4b, c). The repeatability of Eu-MOF for the detection of Cr2O7 2-/CrO4 2- was determined by centrifuging the tested Eu-MOF, washing and air-drying. As shown in Fig. S8, the fluorescence intensity of Eu-MOF remains basically unchanged after five cycles, which indicates that Eu-MOF can also be used to selectively detect Cr2O7 2-/CrO4 2- with good anti-interference ability and recyclability.

As shown in Fig. 4d and 4f, the luminescence intensity of Eu-MOF gradually decreases with the increase of Cr2O7 2-/CrO4 2- concentration. From the fluorescence intensity data of I0/I, it can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of Eu-MOF gradually decreases with the increase of Cr2O7 2-/CrO4 2- concentration in the range of 0–1.0 × 10-5 and 0–1.0 × 10-6M (Fig. 4d, f). The linear correlation coefficients of R2 are both 0.995, and the quenching coefficients (Ksv) were calculated 1.06 × 105 and 1.02 × 106 M−1 (Fig. 4e, h). The LOD of Eu-MOF for Cr2O7 2-/CrO4 2- is 1.32 × 10-8 and 1.39 × 10-9 M, respectively (Fig. S9), which are lower than the most fluorescent sensors for Cr2O7 2-/CrO4 2- reported in recent years (Table S4) [50–52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms of detecting benzaldehyde, Hg2+ and Cr2O7 2-/CrO4 2-
ce:section.ce:para: The main sensing mechanisms reported in literatures are as follows: framework collapse, host-analyte ion exchange, fluorescence resonance energy transfer and competitive absorption. Generally, framework collapse is verified by the PXRD spectra of the samples before and after the detection. Ion exchange is studied by ICP-AES. Fluorescence resonance energy transfer and competitive absorption are verified by the excitation spectrum, emission spectrum of MOF and the UV–vis absorption spectra of the detection object [40,53,54].

For benzaldehyde and Cr2O7 2-/CrO4 2-, there were no changes in the PXRD patterns of Eu-MOF before and after detection of benzaldehyde and Cr2O7 2-/CrO4 2- (Fig. S10a), which showed that the structure of Eu-MOF remained unchanged. Therefore, the mechanism of skeleton collapse is excluded. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. S10b, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of benzaldehyde and Cr2O7 2-/CrO4 2- greatly overlaps the excitation spectrum of Eu-MOF. Therefore, the mechanism of Eu-MOF detecting benzaldehyde and Cr2O7 2-/CrO4 2- is that there is energy competitive absorption between benzaldehyde/Cr2O7 2-/CrO4 2- and Eu-MOF.

While for Hg2+, there is no overlap between the absorption spectrum of Hg2+ and the excitation spectrum of Eu-MOF, indicating that energy competitive absorption could be excluded. (Fig. S11). There are big changes in the PXRD patterns of the Eu-MOF after and before the detection of Hg2+, indicating that the structure of Eu-MOF changed after the detection (Fig. S12). In order to further explain the changes in the structure of the Eu-MOF, the ICP-AES of the supernatant of Eu-MOF soaked in Hg2+ solution for 24 h was measured. The concentration of Hg2+ in the filtrate decreased from 200.59 mg/L to 22.85 mg/L and the concentration of Eu3+ was 14.6 mg/L (Table S4). The experimental data indicate that the Eu-MOF shows efficient removal for Hg2+ and ion exchange did occur between Eu-MOF and Hg2+, leading to changes in the structure of Eu-MOF.

To further investigate the quenching effect, the lifetimes of Eu-MOF at 618 nm before and after the addition of benzaldehyde, Hg2+ and Cr2O7 2-/CrO4 2- (Cr2O7 2- as an example) were determined respectively. The results were as shown in Fig. S13, and the lifetime values were shown in Table S5. The decrease in the lifetimes indicated that the fluorescence quenching must involve the dynamic quenching mechanism [55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the applying feasibility of the Eu-MOF in real samples, the standard addition method was used and tap water, tea and Beijing Wanquan River water were selected. Different amounts of Hg2+ were respectively spiked into the above water samples. As shown in Table 1 , trace amounts of Hg2+ were detected using the Eu-MOF probe and ICP-MS, and the values were close to each other. The Eu-MOF probe displayed high recovery (99.84 to 102.34 %), and low relative standard deviation (0.07–2.01 %) (Fig. S14). It shows that the method has high detection accuracy and practicability. Therefore, the developed Eu-MOF sensing platform showed a high accuracy and strong practicability for the quantitative determination of Hg2+ in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr2O7 2-/CrO4 2- in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water and milk were selected as real samples and the chromium detection was conducted [56]. The relevant experimental results are shown in Table 2 below. The average recovery rate is 99.82 ∼ 102.00 %, and the RSD is 0.28 ∼ 2.66 %, indicating good detection quality and accuracy of Eu-MOF as a fluorescence probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential application as a paper-based sensor
ce:section.ce:para: A fluorescence test paper for benzaldehyde detection was developed, as shown in Fig. 5 a. Soak the free filter paper strip in Eu-MOF suspension and dry it. Repeat this process multiple times to develop a test strip. Under the 365 nm UV lamp, the prepared test strips emits bright red fluorescence. To simulate the complexity of the real environment, the selectivity of the test strips is also studied in the presence of various simulators. As shown in Fig. 5b, various solvents were dropped to the prepared test strips. The fluorescence of the test strips was completely quenched after the addition of benzaldehyde, while no obvious color changes were caused by other solvents. Therefore, the prepared Eu-MOF test strips have the excellent selectivity for visual detecting of benzaldehyde.

Benzaldehyde is volatile. Its vapor is just as toxic as its liquid. In addition, there is no MOF-based sensor reported for gas benzaldehyde detection. Considering these, the prepared Eu-MOF filter paper strips were studied in detecting gas benzaldehyde. When the test strips were exposed to benzaldehyde vapor, the fluorescence quenching was observed after 10 min under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 5c). This method provides broad application prospects for the detection of liquid and gas benzaldehyde.

A portable luminescent paper-based analytical device was developed. The luminescent color of the test strip paper gradually changed from red to colorless under the 365 nm ultraviolet lamp with the addition of different concentrations of Hg2+, which was detected by the naked eye (Fig. 5d). In addition, a mobile application called 'Pixeur' is used to implement intelligent detection, as shown in Fig. 5e. The image can be decomposed into three colors, including red, green and blue, which are usually represented by RGB values [57]. The application can convert the captured color information into RGB values, so as to realize the intelligent and visual detection of Hg2+. Considering the chromaticity range, the blue channel (B value) and red channel (R value) of each image are extracted for subsequent analysis. Fig. 5f shows that the B / R value has a good linear relationship with the concentration of Hg2+ in a wide range of 0–140 μM. According to this linear relationship, the concentration of Hg2+ can be calculated based on the average RGB value obtained. Therefore, this portable smartphone can be used to measure Hg2+. Compared with traditional methods that rely on precision instruments, it can easily achieve real-time quantitative field analysis.

In conclusion, a multifunctional fluorescent probe Eu-MOF was successfully developed for the detection of gas/liquid benzaldehyde, Hg2+ and Cr2O7 2-/CrO4 2- with high sensitivity, selectivity, and anti-interference ability. Convenient test strips based on the Eu-MOF were prepared for visual detection of gas/liquid benzaldehyde. This is the first MOF-based fluorescence probe that can be used for the detection of benzaldehyde in gas and liquid phases. Furthermore, the Eu-MOF was successfully applied for the detection of Hg2+ in real samples such as tap water, tea, and Beijing Wanquan River water with the recoveries of 99.84–102.34 % and the good repeatability (RSD < 2.01 %, n = 3). Therefore, the present work provides a good prospect of the on-site and feasible fluorescence sensor based on Ln-MOF in environmental testing.

Linhan Fan: Investigation, Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Jingyue Zhang: Investigation, Software. Yun Zhao: Investigation, Data curation. Changyan Sun: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition. Wenjun Li: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Zhidong Chang: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project is supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21101013) and Foundation for key young teachers of University of Science and Technology Beijing (2302019JXGGRC-003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109712.

ce:section.ce:para:
